title: ABC谋杀案
summary:
Alexander Bonaparte Cast, who suffers from epilepsy, collapses and faints while walking toward the Anderson Police Station. 

On a clear November day, Dr. Thompson, Inspector Japp, and Hastings visit Hercule Poirot, who is currently suffering from bronchitis. Japp informs the group that the case against Cast is proceeding to trial. Japp notes that the prosecutor, Lucas, may attempt to use an insanity defense, though Poirot argues that an insane killer would not necessarily be acquitted.

Dr. Thompson disputes the idea that Cast could have committed the Bexhill murder while in a trance-like epileptic state, pointing out that the premeditated letters written by Cast suggest the crimes were carefully planned. Poirot remains preoccupied with why Cast specifically addressed those letters to him. Thompson suggests that Cast’s use of grand names like "Alexander" and "Napoleon" indicates a desire for a grand rival, implying that Poirot is the intended opponent.

Japp introduces a significant complication regarding an alibi provided by a mining engineer named Strange. Strange claims he played dominoes with Cast at the White Cross Hotel in Eastbourne until midnight on July 25th. If this is true, Cast could not have traveled the fourteen miles to Bexhill to murder Betty Barnard between midnight and one in the morning. While Cast's guilt in the murders of Teng Carr, Chester, and Anderswal is considered certain due to physical evidence, the Bexhill murder remains a logistical puzzle. Poirot concludes the case is not truly closed because the paradoxical nature of Cast—a mixture of cunning and stupidity, and coldness and nobility—remains unexplained.
